Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator 
\-Tomen For: 
April 12, 1976 
8913 West Olympic Boulevard 
Beverly Hills, California. 90211 
Dear Ms. Cooperman: 
This letter is in response to your inquiry of April 7, 
1976 concerning your calendar of events mailing. 
The Commission considered at its meeting last week a 
regulation redefining the term "expenditure" in light of 
the U. S. Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo. 
Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation. 
it was the members' consensus that expenditures for commu-
nications which do not expressly advocate the election or 
defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not 
reportable by groups such as wBMiH FOR. 
Therefore you do 
not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report 
the costs of printing and mailing the calendars. 
I will keep you informed of the Commission's actions 
regarding the applicability of disclosure requirements to 
WOMEN FOR:. 
'If you have further questions. please contact our staff 
aga.in. 
Sincerely, 
Nan Hambleton 
Special Compliance Representative 
NH/bw

HANDGUN CO'NTaO'L INrTIATlVE TO' HALT M1ERICA'S GUN TRAGEDY 
We are enclosing a petition for the Handgun Control Initiative for 
the November ballot, for you to gather signatures. 
In addition to WO'MEN FO'R:, this initiative is supported by the Los 
Angeles Police Commission. Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors, 
Los Angeles Board of Education, and many local state and federal 
legislators and members of the Los Angeles City Council. 
Here is your chance as WOMEN FO'R: members to make a real impact on 
the safety of all our lives. 
We CP~ stop the proliferation of hand-
guns in California (2~ million sold yearly in the United States). 
We CAN make our homes, schools and streets safer. 
The United States 
now has the highest homicide rate of any country. 
Handguns serve 
no purpose except to kill. 
WOMEN FO'R: HAS M.AJ)E A COMMITI1ENT TO' GATHER SIGNATURES FO'R 
THIS INITIATIVE - WE NEED YOUR HELP TO' CIRCULATE PETITIO'NS! 
In circulating the petition, please remember: 
1. 
You must be a registered voter in the county in which you cir-
culate the petition. 
2. 
Those Signing the petition must be registered to vote in the 
county in which they are signing. 
3. Voters must sign the petition exactly as they are registered 
to vote. 
(Mr., Mrs., Miss, Ms. no longer necessary.) 
4. Sign a petition yourself, and don't forget to sign all the pet-
itions that you circulate, as the circulator, at the bottom. 
5. 
Send petition v1ith as many signatures as you can get to the 
Coalition for Handgun Control. 8455 BeveIly Blvd., Los Angeles, 
90Q4§. 
6. 
For information or more petitions, call the Handgun Control 
Coalition office, 653-1166. 
PLEASE HELP! 
WE NEED 500,000' SIGNATURES BY ~iAY 26th TO' QUALIFY THIS INITL~TlVE! 
If you can give a few hours of your time 
any other are~, cal 
the WOMEN FOR: office,

INITIATIVE 
The Attorney General of California 
proposed measure: 
BE SUBMITTED DIRECTLY 
the following title and summary 
chief purpose and points of 
HANDGUN REGULATION: INITIATIVE STATUTE. Permits surreuder of handguns prior to December 
31, 1977; directs Legislature to provide 'compensation therefor. Effective January 1, 1978, prohibits pur-
chase, transfer, or possession of operable handguns, and handgun ammuuition, with prescribed exceptions, 
including possession by police, antique gun collectors, gun club members, security guards and gun dealers. 
Creates special fund from fines for handgun crimes to compensate for surrendered guns and for appro-
priations from fund to indemnify crime victims, after such compensation is paid. Imposes state administered 
handgun licensing and registration requirements. Repeals local authority to license sale, carrying of hand-
guns. Prescribes penalties for violations. Financial impact: Adoption of this measure will result in increased 
state and local one time costs of at least $3,783,000 which sum does not include amounts necessary to 
reimburse owners of surrendered handguns. 
All signers of this petition must be registered to vote in _____ ~_

The Attorney General shali annually review and evaluate such reports to ascertain

Article 5. Monetary Institutions 
]2080. It is the intent of this article to permit the use of bandguns by monetary 
institutions in order to provide for the safe deposit, storage, and transportation of 
money, treaSUfe, buBion~ stock certificates, bond:" or other negotiabIc instruments. 
12080.1. For the purposes of this article, "department" means the Department of 
Consumer Affairs and "director" means the Director of the Department of Consumer 
Affairs. 
12081. The department, upon proper application, shall issue or reneW a license 
to purchase handguns to any monetary institution which complies with the require~ 
ments of this article and the regulations promulgated by the director. 
12082. The director shall promulgate regulations to do the following: 
(a) To provide for adequate security against theft, misappropriation or misuse 
of any handgun owned by a monetary institution or possessed by its authorized 
employees. 
(b) To provide that employees of the monetary institution who will possess 
handguns are trained in the proper use and safety of handguns. 
For the 
purposes of this subdivision, the course of training prescribed by the department 
pursuant to Section 7514.1 of the Business and Professions Code shall constitute 
the minimum standards for training required by this subdivision. The director 
may promulgate stricter standards for training than those described in this 
subdivision. 
(c) To provide for adequate insurance against damage or injury caused by any 
handgun owned by a monetary institution or possessed by any employee thereof, 
including a requirement that each monetary institution maintain insurance against 
such damage or injury in such amounts as the director shall prescribe. 
(d) To prescribe the maximum number of handguns that may be owned by each 
monetary institution, bearing a reasonable relationship to the number of guards 
and messengers entitled to possess handguns who are employed by such monetary 
institution. 
(e) To assure that handguns owned or possessed pursuant to tbis article are 
used in a manner consistent with the public safety. 
(t) Such other regulations as the director deems to be in the interest of public 
safety. 
12083. Any monetary institution may apply to the department for a Iiccnse to 
purchase handguns for the purpose of guarding money, treasure, beillion. stock 
certificates, bonds, or other negotiable instruments. 
.

such applications. Such applications shall include the name, address, and location 
of the principal place of business of the monetary institution, and the name, signature.

Article 10. Surrender of Handglllls 
12140. Beginning on March 1, 1977, and not later than December 31, 1977, any 
person having a handgun, except an antique handgun, in his possession or under his 
control may ~urrcnder the same to the sheriff of any county or the chief or other 
head of any municipal police department of any city or county, or city and county. 
Upon surrender of sllch handgun, the authority to whom the handgun has been 
surrendered shall issue to such person a receipt for the handgun, which shall include 
a description of the handgun and weh other information as the Attorney General 
shall prescribe by regulation. 
12141. (a) In addition to all other penalties provided by law, any person who is 
convicted of violating Sections 12023, 12023.5, 12024, 12025, 12160, or any of 
them, or, where the firearm involved is a handgun, Sections 12021 or 12021.5, 
shall pay a fine of two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) which shall be 
paid over to the Controller of the State of California and segregated by him in a 
special fund. 
ib) The Legislature shall provide, by enactment effective not later than August 
31, 1978, for payment for the surrender of handguns pursuant to this article. The 
amoun! and methods of payment shall be determined by the Legislature, The funds 
in the special fund described in subdivision (a) shall be annually transferred into 
the general fund and applied to repayment of the sums paid for the surrender of 
handguns pursuant to this article until slIch time as the total amount appropriated 
from the general fund by the Legislature to pay for such surrender has been repaid 
to the general fund. Thereafter, the balance of such special fund, and all fines 
collected shall be deposited in the indemnity fund in the State Treasury, the 
proceeds of which shall be available for appropriation by the Legislature to 
indemnify victims of crime who tile claims pursuant to Article 1 (commencing 
with Section 13959) of Chapter 5 of Part 4 of Division 3 of the Government 
Code, 
12142. No payment shall be provided for any handgun surrendered unless at the 
time of surrender, the person surrendering the same shall have supplied to the 
authority acceptint; such handgun both of the following; 
(a) Proof that the handgun was purchased prior to November 2, 1976. Such 
proof shall consist of (1) a suitable receipt identifying the handgun and estab-
lishing that such handgun was purchased prior to such date, or (2) a declaration 
under penalty of perjury containing facts showing that the receipt described in 
paragraph (l) of this subdivision was never issued or has been lost and that such 
handgun was purchased prior to November 2, 1976. 
(b) Proof that such handgun was in California on or before November 2,1976. 
Such proof shall consist of (I) the receipt described in paragraph (1) of sub-
division (a) if stich receipt shows that the handgun was purchased in California, 
or (2) a declaratIOn under penalty of perjury containing facts showing that the 
handgun was in California on or before November 2, 1976.

Article 12. Miscellaneous Provisions 
12160. (a) Any person who, for the purpose of causing to be issued, amended, 
or renewed any license under this chapter. or for the purpose of causing a handgun

sections.conclusion: Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as Women For. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Women For, a political committee, must report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars of events and comply with mass mailing requirements under the Fair Political Practices Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Women For does not have to report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars or comply with mass mailing requirements, as these communications do not expressly advocate for or against a clearly identified candidate or measure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Women For, a political committee, must report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars of events and comply with mass mailing requirements under the Fair Political Practices Act.

CONCLUSION: Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as Women For. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars.
embedding.first_500_words: Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator \-Tomen For: April 12, 1976 8913 West Olympic Boulevard Beverly Hills, California. 90211 Dear Ms. Cooperman: This letter is in response to your inquiry of April 7, 1976 concerning your calendar of events mailing. The Commission considered at its meeting last week a regulation redefining the term "expenditure" in light of the U. S. Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo. Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation. it was the members' consensus that expenditures for commu- nications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as wBMiH FOR. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars. I will keep you informed of the Commission's actions regarding the applicability of disclosure requirements to WOMEN FOR:. 'If you have further questions. please contact our staff aga.in. Sincerely, Nan Hambleton Special Compliance Representative NH/bw i HANDGUN CO'NTaO'L INrTIATlVE TO' HALT M1ERICA'S GUN TRAGEDY We are enclosing a petition for the Handgun Control Initiative for the November ballot, for you to gather signatures. In addition to WO'MEN FO'R:, this initiative is supported by the Los Angeles Police Commission. Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors, Los Angeles Board of Education, and many local state and federal legislators and members of the Los Angeles City Council. Here is your chance as WOMEN FO'R: members to make a real impact on the safety of all our lives. We CP~ stop the proliferation of hand- guns in California (2~ million sold yearly in the United States). We CAN make our homes, schools and streets safer. The United States now has the highest homicide rate of any country. Handguns serve no purpose except to kill. WOMEN FO'R: HAS M.AJ)E A COMMITI1ENT TO' GATHER SIGNATURES FO'R THIS INITIATIVE - WE NEED YOUR HELP TO' CIRCULATE PETITIO'NS! In circulating the petition, please remember: 1. You must be a registered voter in the county in which you cir- culate the petition. 2. Those Signing the petition must be registered to vote in the county in which they are signing. 3. Voters must sign the petition exactly as they are registered to vote. (Mr., Mrs., Miss, Ms. no longer necessary.) 4. Sign a petition yourself, and don't forget to sign all the pet- itions that you circulate, as the circulator, at the bottom. 5. Send petition v1ith as many signatures as you can get to the Coalition for Handgun Control. 8455 BeveIly Blvd., Los Angeles, 90Q4§. 6. For information or more petitions, call the Handgun Control Coalition office, 653-1166. PLEASE HELP! WE NEED 500,000' SIGNATURES BY ~iAY 26th TO' QUALIFY THIS INITL~TlVE! If you can give a few hours of your time any other are~, cal the WOMEN FOR: office, t Century 11, to R DE 1 R Donations & Dues ....... ......... . .. Abzug Luncheon ..................... . Education Seminar ................... . Kassorla (Evening Group) ............. . McWilliams Event
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter from April 12, 1976 to Roslyn Cooperman addressing whether Women For must report calendar mailing costs and comply with mass mailing requirements under campaign finance law. The FPPC advised that such expenditures need not be reported as they do not constitute reportable expenditures under the Buckley v. Valeo standard.
